reference_observations: The stronger reference posts worked because they were concrete, personal, and anchored in real moments: a build, a launch, a talk, a room full of engineers. But they mostly stayed at the level of announcement, event recap, or broad excitement. What they missed was the specific human texture in this Raw Idea: teaching as a TA, the Nexus workshop with Mason, Melissa’s AI session, the surprise Tiki room, live music with game visuals, glow-in-the-dark cotton candy, and the bigger systems takeaway that durable execution is becoming core AI infrastructure. This post can differentiate by being more grounded in what was actually seen and felt while still extracting one sharp lesson for engineers building AI in production.

variant_1.angle: Replay 2026 convinced me durable AI orchestration is becoming infrastructure, not a niche
variant_1.post:
I just got back from Temporal Replay 2026.

What stood out wasn't just the scale.
It was the clarity.

More than 2,000 engineers showed up to talk about durable distributed systems, agentic AI, and what it takes to run AI operations in production.

That matters.

A lot of AI discourse still lives at the model layer.
Bigger context windows. Better evals. New model releases.

But the hallway conversations at Replay kept landing on a different problem:

How do you make AI systems survive reality?

- retries without duplicate side effects
- long-running tasks that survive deploys
- human-in-the-loop steps
- resumability after failures
- auditability when an agent makes a decision

That was the real center of gravity.

I was lucky to help TA two workshops: the Nexus workshop led by Mason, and Melissa's AI session.

And the same pattern kept showing up there too.

Once you move past demos, AI stops being mainly a prompting problem.
It becomes an orchestration problem.

Seeing companies like Netflix and OpenAI talk about using Temporal to scale AI operations made that feel a lot less theoretical.

My biggest takeaway from Replay:

**Durable execution is quietly becoming part of the AI stack.**

Also: this may have been the most unexpectedly fun systems conference I've attended.
A secret Tiki room, live music with retro game visuals, and glow-in-the-dark cotton candy is not the conference combo I would have predicted.

Huge thanks to Temporal, Mason, Melissa, and everyone I got to learn from.

I left with a notebook full of ideas and a stronger belief that reliable AI will be built by engineers who care about orchestration as much as models.

If you're building AI in production, what failure mode are you spending the most time on right now?
variant_1.intended_audience: Senior engineers, staff engineers, and AI infrastructure builders working on production agents and distributed systems.
variant_1.why_it_may_perform: It combines event energy with a concrete industry takeaway, uses named entities from the conference, and reframes the conference as evidence of a broader infrastructure shift rather than a generic recap.
variant_1.risks: Could feel slightly broad if readers want more specific technical examples; depends on the audience already caring about orchestration.
variant_1.what_this_brings_vs_references: It turns the conference recap into a sharper production-systems thesis anchored in specific moments from Replay 2026.

variant_2.angle: The best part of Replay 2026 was the people behind the systems
variant_2.post:
I went to Temporal Replay 2026 expecting to learn a lot about durable execution and AI orchestration.

I did.

But the part I'm leaving most grateful for is the people.

Replay was a beautiful gathering of 2,000+ engineers trying to build AI systems that are not just impressive, but dependable.

I got to support two workshops as a teaching assistant:

- the Nexus workshop led by Mason
- Melissa's AI session

There's something special about being in a room where people are asking real questions instead of performative ones.

Not "what's the hottest framework?"

More like:

- how do you recover long-running work?
- how do you make agents observable?
- how do you keep AI operations correct under failure?

Those are my favorite engineering conversations.

And outside the sessions, Replay somehow managed to feel both deeply technical and genuinely joyful.

A secret Tiki room.
Live music.
Glow-in-the-dark cotton candy.

That combination should not work.
It absolutely worked.

I also loved hearing how companies like Netflix and OpenAI are using Temporal to scale real AI operations. It's easy to talk about agents in the abstract. It's much better to hear how teams are actually running them.

Huge thanks to Temporal for hosting, and to Mason, Melissa, and everyone I met over the last few days.

I learned a lot.
But more importantly, I left energized by the kind of people building this space.

The future of AI infrastructure will be shaped by human beings who care about correctness, reliability, and each other.

Who did you meet at Replay 2026 that changed how you think about the space?
variant_2.intended_audience: Engineers who value community, conference networking, and the human side of building reliable AI systems.
variant_2.why_it_may_perform: It directly embodies the gratitude and connection emotional core, which the raw idea strongly signals, while still keeping enough technical vocabulary to stay relevant to the ICP.
variant_2.risks: Less overtly authoritative than a lesson-driven post; some readers may see it as softer than a technical audience prefers.
variant_2.what_this_brings_vs_references: It leans into gratitude and human connection more directly than the references while staying grounded in production-AI conversations.

variant_3.angle: What I learned TAing the Nexus and AI workshops at Replay 2026
variant_3.post:
I spent part of Temporal Replay 2026 helping as a TA in two workshops:

- the Nexus workshop led by Mason
- Melissa's AI workshop

That gave me a better signal than the keynotes.

You learn a lot about a technology by watching where engineers get stuck.

A few patterns kept showing up in the room:

1. Engineers are excited about AI agents.
But the hard questions start immediately after the demo works.

2. The real implementation pain is usually orchestration, not generation.
People ask about retries, state, resumability, timeouts, compensation, and observability far more than prompt wording.

3. Durable systems thinking is becoming an AI skill.
Not just a distributed systems skill.

That was the strongest theme I saw across Replay.

The talks from companies like Netflix and OpenAI reinforced it too. The interesting part wasn't "we use AI."
It was "here's how we run AI operations at scale without losing control of the system."

And honestly, I love that this is where the conversation is going.

We've had plenty of time in the industry focused on model magic.
Now more engineers are asking the production questions.

That's healthy.

Also, Replay had some of the best conference energy I've seen in a while: a secret Tiki room, live music with giant game visuals, and glow-in-the-dark cotton candy.

Huge thanks to Temporal, Mason, Melissa, and everyone who came through the workshops.

My biggest lesson from TAing:

**The future AI engineer probably looks a lot like a distributed systems engineer.**

If you've taught a workshop before, what questions from attendees told you where the industry is really headed?
variant_3.intended_audience: Hands-on engineers building agents, workflows, and AI platforms who care about where real implementation pain shows up.
variant_3.why_it_may_perform: It offers authority without sounding promotional by using the TA vantage point, extracts concrete patterns from workshop interactions, and ties them back to the broader conference narrative.
variant_3.risks: Less emotionally warm than the reflection variant; may underuse the celebratory side of the raw idea if paired with the wrong image.
variant_3.what_this_brings_vs_references: It uses a more insider, operator-level vantage point than the references by focusing on what engineers actually struggled with in the workshops.

variant_4.angle: Replay 2026 was a rare conference that got both the systems thinking and the atmosphere right
variant_4.post:
Most engineering conferences get one of two things right:

The content.
Or the atmosphere.

Temporal Replay 2026 somehow got both.

On one side, you had serious conversations about durable execution, distributed systems, and AI operations.

On the other, you had a secret Tiki room, live music playing in front of giant retro game visuals, and glow-in-the-dark cotton candy.

That contrast sounds silly on paper.
But it captured something real about the event.

The future of AI infrastructure is being built by people who care deeply about correctness and reliability.
That doesn't make the work any less human.

I was lucky to support two workshops as a TA: the Nexus workshop led by Mason, and Melissa's AI workshop.

What I appreciated most was how grounded the conversations were.
Not hype.
Not vague "agents will change everything" claims.

Just engineers asking the production questions:

- how do we recover work after failure?
- how do we coordinate long-running AI tasks?
- how do we make AI operations observable and auditable?

Hearing companies like Netflix and OpenAI share how they're using Temporal at scale made that even more concrete.

Huge thanks to Temporal for hosting such a thoughtful event.

I left with new ideas, new connections, and a stronger sense that durable orchestration is going to matter a lot more over the next few years.

Also, every conference should consider adding glow-in-the-dark cotton candy.

What conference have you attended that actually got both the technical depth and the community feeling right?
variant_4.intended_audience: Engineers who attend conferences, care about technical depth, and appreciate authentic community over hype.
variant_4.why_it_may_perform: It has a strong contrast-based hook, memorable sensory details, and a more distinctive angle than a standard recap.
variant_4.risks: Slightly less authority-forward than the TA or infrastructure thesis variants; could read as event commentary more than engineering insight.
variant_4.what_this_brings_vs_references: It adds sensory specificity and atmosphere that the references only hinted at, making the post more vivid and memorable.
